---
title: "From Geostatistics to Health Outcomes:<br>Open-Source Tools for Environmental Epidemiology"
subtitle: "Environmental and Occupational Epidemiology Seminar"
author: "Kyle P. Messier, PhD"
institute: "Stadtman Tenure-Track Investigator<br>Division of Translational Toxicology & Biostatistics and Computational Biology Branch<br>National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    chalkboard: false
    logo: ""
    footer: "Kyle P. Messier, PhD | NIEHS"
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    highlight-style: github
    code-overflow: wrap
    fig-align: center
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    scrollable: true
    smaller: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Background {.section-title background-color="#1a3a5c"}

## Career Overview

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**A path through geostatistics, exposure science, and open-source tools**

- 🎓 **PhD** — UNC Chapel Hill, Environmental Sciences & Engineering
- 🔬 **Postdoc** — Environmental Defense Fund & UT Austin
- 🏔️ **Faculty** — Oregon State University (K99/R00)
- 🏛️ **NIEHS** — Stadtman Tenure-Track Investigator
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[Career timeline graphic — placeholder]*
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Introduce the narrative arc: each career stage added a new tool or method that feeds into the current research program.
:::

## UNC Chapel Hill — PhD

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="65%"}
**Department of Environmental Sciences & Engineering**

- Focus: **geostatistics** and spatiotemporal exposure modeling
- Developed scalable methods for large geospatial datasets
- Key insight: land-use regression (LUR) models need to account for spatially correlated residuals
- Collaboration with **Matthias Katzfuss** → Vecchia approximation for GP scalability

> *Messier & Katzfuss (2020). Scalable penalized spatiotemporal land-use regression for ground-level nitrogen dioxide. JRSS-A*
:::
::: {.column width="35%"}
*[NO₂ concentration map — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## Postdoc — EDF & UT Austin

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Mobile Monitoring & Urban Air Quality Mapping**

- **Environmental Defense Fund** + **UT Austin** collaboration
- Deployed mobile monitoring platforms to measure near-road air quality at high spatial resolution
- Mapped intra-urban variability in NO₂, PM₂.₅, black carbon
- Demonstrated that fixed-site monitors miss substantial intra-urban gradients
- Contributed to Google Street View–EDF collaborative campaigns
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[Mobile monitoring map / campaign image — placeholder]*
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Highlight the practical motivation: if you don't measure at fine resolution, you miss health-relevant exposure gradients.
:::

## Oregon State University — K99/R00

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="65%"}
**NIH K99/R00 Pathway to Independence Award**

- Built independent research program bridging **geostatistics** and **environmental epidemiology**
- Developed multi-pollutant spatiotemporal frameworks
- Began building open-source R tools for the exposure modeling community
- Focus: scalable methods applicable to national-scale, multi-year exposure assessment
:::
::: {.column width="35%"}
*[K99 project schematic — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## NIEHS — Stadtman Tenure-Track Investigator

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Spatiotemporal Exposures and Toxicology (SET) Group**

- **Division of Translational Toxicology** — Predictive Toxicology Branch
- **Biostatistics and Computational Biology Branch**
- Part of the NIH **Stadtman Investigator** program
- Research focus:
  - Scalable spatiotemporal exposure models
  - Open-source software for the epi community
  - Linking exposures to health outcomes at scale
  - CHORDS: Connecting Health Outcomes Research Data Systems
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[SET group / NIEHS logo — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## Research Thread

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment}
**The core question:** How do we accurately measure and model environmental exposures at the scale needed for epidemiology?
:::
::: {.fragment}
**The approach:** Build statistically rigorous, computationally scalable, and *open-source* tools.
:::
:::

. . .

| Stage | Tool | Application |
|-------|------|-------------|
| Geostatistics | **PrestoGP** | Spatiotemporal exposure modeling |
| Data infrastructure | **amadeus** | Environmental data access & processing |
| Ensemble modeling | **beethoven** | National air quality prediction |
| Health outcomes | **hcup_ap** | Wildfire smoke × immune disease |

---

# PrestoGP {.section-title background-color="#2d6a4f"}

## Why Spatiotemporal GP Models?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Limitations of standard LUR:**

- Assumes spatially independent residuals ❌
- Doesn't scale to millions of observations ❌
- Variable selection treated separately from model fitting ❌
- Struggles with missing data / censoring ❌
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we need:**

- Spatiotemporal covariance structure ✅
- Scalable computation for big data ✅
- Simultaneous penalized variable selection ✅
- Principled handling of LOD / missingness ✅
:::
:::

## The Vecchia Approximation

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**General Vecchia approximation** *(Katzfuss & Guinness 2021)*

- Approximates the full GP likelihood using **conditional independence**:
$$p(\mathbf{y}) \approx \prod_{i=1}^n p(y_i \mid y_{c(i)})$$
where $c(i)$ is a small set of "neighbors" for observation $i$

- Reduces computation from $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ → $\mathcal{O}(nm^3)$ where $m \ll n$
- Enables GP inference on **millions** of spatio-temporal observations
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[Vecchia neighbor diagram — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## Penalized Regression + GP

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**PrestoGP model** *(Messier & Katzfuss 2020, JRSS-A)*

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \mathbf{w} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$$

- $\mathbf{w}$: spatiotemporal GP with Matérn covariance
- $\boldsymbol{\beta}$: simultaneously estimated via penalized likelihood
  - LASSO / SCAD / MCP penalties available
- Scalable via Vecchia approximation
- Joint estimation — no "plug-in" residuals
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[Model diagram — placeholder]*
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
> **Key innovation:** Variable selection and GP estimation happen *simultaneously*, avoiding bias from two-stage approaches.
:::

## PrestoGP R Package

```r
# Install from GitHub
devtools::install_github("NIEHS/PrestoGP")

# Fit a Vecchia model
model <- new("VecchiaModel", n_neighbors = 10)
model <- prestogp_fit(model, y, X, locs)

# Predict at new locations
yhat <- prestogp_predict(model, X_new, locs_new)
```

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Model classes:**

- `VecchiaModel` — univariate spatiotemporal
- `FullModel` — exact GP (small data)
- `MultivariateVecchiaModel` — multiple outcomes
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Key features:**

- Spatial, spatiotemporal, space/elevation
- Flexible scaling parameters per dimension
- `impute.y = TRUE` for missing outcomes
- `lod` argument for censored data
:::
:::

## Multivariate Spatiotemporal Models

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Multivariate Matérn cross-covariance** *(Apanasovich et al. 2012)*

- Models multiple pollutants *simultaneously*
- Valid cross-covariance function for any number of components
- Captures cross-pollutant spatial dependence
- Each outcome gets its own LUR predictors

```r
model_mv <- new("MultivariateVecchiaModel",
                n_neighbors = 10)
model_mv <- prestogp_fit(model_mv,
                         y = list(y1, y2),
                         X = list(X1, X2),
                         locs = list(locs, locs))
```
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[Cross-covariance schematic — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## Missing Data & Limit of Detection

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Two imputation modes:**

1. **MCAR missingness** — impute within GP framework
2. **LOD censoring** — impute values below detection limit

```r
# MCAR imputation
model <- prestogp_fit(model, y, X, locs,
                      impute.y = TRUE)

# LOD imputation
lod_val <- quantile(y, 0.1)
model <- prestogp_fit(model, y_censored, X, locs,
                      impute.y = TRUE,
                      lod = lod_val)
```
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Why this matters:**

- Many environmental measurements are censored below detection limits
- Naive approaches (substitution with LOD/2) introduce bias
- GP framework allows principled probabilistic imputation
:::
:::

## Application: NO₂ Mapping

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Messier & Katzfuss (2020), JRSS-A**

- Continental US NO₂ ground-level concentrations
- Land-use regression predictors: road density, population, NDVI, traffic, etc.
- Vecchia-GP captures spatial autocorrelation after LUR adjustment
- Penalized regression selects relevant predictors automatically
- Better cross-validation performance vs. standard LUR
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
*[Predicted NO₂ surface map — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## Application: Chlorotriazine Groundwater

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**In Progress — Messier, Song, Wheeler et al.**

> *Multivariate and Censored Geospatial Models for External Exposomics of Data-Sparse Chemicals: US Chlorotriazine Groundwater Distributions 1990–2022*

- Chlorotriazines (atrazine, simazine, etc.) are common herbicides
- Groundwater monitoring data is spatially sparse + heavily censored
- **Multivariate** model: capture cross-chemical dependence
- **LOD imputation**: handle censoring below detection
- National prediction surface at 1 km resolution
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[Chlorotriazine map — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## PrestoGP: Open Science

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**`github.com/NIEHS/PrestoGP`**

- R package with full documentation
- `prestogp_fit()` / `prestogp_predict()` API
- Automated CI/CD: R-CMD-check, unit tests, coverage, lint
- pkgdown documentation site
- MIT/GPL-3 license

**Active development:**

- Multivariate censored models
- Exposomics applications
- Performance improvements for very large datasets
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
[![PrestoGP](https://github.com/NIEHS/PrestoGP/actions/workflows/check-standard.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/NIEHS/PrestoGP)

*[PrestoGP pkgdown screenshot — placeholder]*
:::
:::

---

# amadeus + beethoven {.section-title background-color="#6b3fa0"}

## Motivation: Data Infrastructure for Exposure Science

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**The problem:**

- Environmental health research requires dozens of heterogeneous data sources
- Each source: unique file formats, APIs, naming conventions, coordinate systems
- Researchers spend enormous time on data wrangling before any analysis
- Results are often not reproducible across teams or HPC systems

**The solution:**

- `amadeus`: standardized access and processing
- `beethoven`: reproducible ensemble modeling pipeline
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**CHORDS Program (NIEHS)**

> *Connecting Health Outcomes Research Data Systems*

Building data infrastructure for patient-centered outcomes research on environment and health.
:::
:::

## amadeus: Overview

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**A Mechanism for Data, Environments, and User Setup**

- R package on **CRAN**: `install.packages("amadeus")`
- **17+ environmental data sources** standardized under one API
- Three-function workflow:
  1. `download_data()` — fetch from source repositories
  2. `process_covariates()` — import, clean, standardize CRS + layer names
  3. `calculate_covariates()` — extract at point locations (with optional buffer)
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[amadeus hex logo]*
:::
:::

> **Cite:** Manware et al. (2025). *Environmental Modelling & Software*, 186, 106352.

## amadeus: Data Sources

| Source | Type | Genre |
|--------|------|-------|
| EPA AQS | CSV | Air Pollution |
| MODIS (NASA) | HDF | Atmosphere / Land Use |
| MERRA-2 (NASA) | netCDF | Meteorology |
| NARR (NOAA) | netCDF | Meteorology |
| GridMet / TerraClimate | netCDF | Climate |
| NLCD (MRLC) | GeoTIFF | Land Use |
| HMS Fire & Smoke (NOAA) | Shapefile | Wildfire Smoke |
| GMTED2010 (USGS) | Grid | Elevation |
| Population Density (NASA SEDAC) | GeoTIFF | Demography |
| EPA NEI / TRI | CSV | Emissions |
| EPA Ecoregions | Shapefile | Climate Regions |
| *+ more* | | |

## amadeus: Workflow in Practice

```r
# 1. Download NARR snow water equivalent
download_data(
  dataset_name = "narr", year = 2022,
  variable = "weasd", directory_to_save = "/data/narr/",
  acknowledgement = TRUE, download = TRUE
)

# 2. Process to standardized SpatRaster
weasd <- process_covariates(
  covariate = "narr", date = c("2022-01-01", "2022-01-05"),
  variable = "weasd", path = "/data/narr/weasd/"
)

# 3. Extract at monitoring site locations
covars <- calculate_covariates(
  covariate = "narr", from = weasd,
  locs = aqs_sites, locs_id = "site_id", radius = 0
)
```

Returns `data.frame` / `sf` / `SpatVector` ready for modeling.

## beethoven: Overview

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**B**uilding an **E**xtensible, r**E**producible, **T**est-driven, **H**armonized, **O**pen-source, **V**ersioned, **EN**semble model for air quality

- R package + `{targets}` reproducible pipeline
- National-scale daily **PM₂.₅** predictions
- Stacked ensemble: base learners + meta learner
- Runs on NIEHS HPC (SLURM + Apptainer containers)
- Fully versioned, tested, open source
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[beethoven hex logo]*
:::
:::

## beethoven: Pipeline Architecture

```
amadeus (data access)
    │
    ▼
[Download: EPA AQS, MODIS, MERRA-2, NARR, NLCD, HMS, ...]
    │
    ▼
[Covariate Calculation at AQS sites & prediction grid]
    │
    ▼
[Base Learners: LightGBM, Neural Net, Regularized Regression, ...]
    │
    ▼
[Meta Learner: stacked ensemble]
    │
    ▼
National daily PM₂.₅ predictions + uncertainty
```

*Pipeline managed by `{targets}` — every step cached and reproducible*

## beethoven: Ensemble Model

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Stacked ensemble via `{tidymodels}`**

- **Base learners:**
  - LightGBM (gradient boosting)
  - Neural networks (via `{brulee}` / `{torch}`)
  - Regularized regression (glmnet)
  - Support vector machines
- **Meta learner:** learns to combine base learner predictions
- Spatial cross-validation via `{spatialsample}`
- Hyperparameter tuning via `{finetune}`
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**HPC infrastructure:**

- `Apptainer` container images (>5 GB each)
- SLURM batch jobs per pipeline stage
- `{crew}` + `{crew.cluster}` for distributed computing
- `{targets}` handles dependency graph + caching
:::
:::

## beethoven: Reproducibility & Open Science

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**`github.com/NIEHS/beethoven`**

- Fully open source (MIT license)
- 100% `{targets}`-managed pipeline — no manual steps
- Automated CI: R-CMD-check, unit tests (testthat), coverage, lint
- Containerized for reproducibility across systems
- pkgdown documentation

**`github.com/NIEHS/amadeus`**

- On CRAN
- Peer-reviewed publication
- Extensive vignettes for each data source
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[beethoven visnetwork pipeline diagram — placeholder]*

*`targets::tar_visnetwork()`*
:::
:::

---

# hcup_ap {.section-title background-color="#c1440e"}

## Closing the Loop: Exposures → Health Outcomes

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Research question:**

> What is the association between **wildfire smoke PM₂.₅** concentrations and **immune-mediated disease** hospitalizations in the United States?

**Why this matters:**

- Wildfire smoke is a growing public health threat
- Immune-mediated diseases (asthma, COPD, autoimmune) may be particularly sensitive
- Administrative health data provides population-level coverage
- Links directly to beethoven-derived exposure estimates
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
*[Wildfire smoke + hospital icon — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## HCUP: The Health Data

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP)**

- AHRQ-administered, multi-state database
- **ED visits** + **inpatient hospitalizations**
- Aggregated to **Zip Code Tabulation Area (ZCTA)** level
- ICD-9 and ICD-10 diagnosis codes

**Phenotype classification:**

- Algorithm maps ICD-9/10 codes → phenotype categories
- Immune-mediated phenotypes: asthma, COPD, autoimmune conditions, allergic disease, etc.
- Validated phenotyping approach
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[HCUP data flowchart — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## Exposure: Wildfire Smoke PM₂.₅

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Multi-source exposure harmonization at ZCTA level:**

- **NOAA HMS** Fire and Smoke Product — smoke presence/intensity
- **beethoven**-derived PM₂.₅ estimates — daily concentrations
- **EPA AQS** ground-level monitoring — validation
- Aggregated from grid to ZCTA using area-weighted mean
- Daily time series, multi-year coverage

**Additional covariates:**

- Social Deprivation Index
- RUCA urbanicity codes
- Temperature, humidity (NARR)
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
*[Smoke + PM₂.₅ map over US — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## Statistical Model: Bayesian Poisson + DLNM

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Ecological time-series analysis**

$$\log(E[Y_{zt}]) = \log(N_{zt}) + \mathbf{cb}(\text{PM}_{zt}, \text{lag}) \cdot \boldsymbol{\beta} + \mathbf{s}(\text{time}) + \mathbf{X}_{zt}\boldsymbol{\gamma}$$

- $Y_{zt}$: hospital visits in ZCTA $z$ on day $t$
- **DLNM**: cross-basis captures exposure-lag-response surface
- Bayesian Poisson regression: full uncertainty propagation
- Random effects for ZCTA-level heterogeneity
- Outputs: **relative risks** and **attributable numbers**
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[DLNM exposure-lag-response surface — placeholder]*
:::
:::

## Key Results

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Findings** *(placeholder — to be filled in)*

- Association between wildfire smoke PM₂.₅ and [phenotype] ED visits
- Distributed lag effects peaking at lag [X] days
- Attributable numbers during high smoke events
- Effect modification by urbanicity / social deprivation
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
*[Forest plot of relative risks by phenotype — placeholder]*

*[Exposure-lag surface heatmap — placeholder]*
:::
:::

::: {.callout-note}
Results and figures to be added as analysis finalizes.
:::

## hcup_ap: Pipeline & Data Management

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**`github.com/NIEHS/hcup_ap`**

- `{targets}` pipeline: `_targets_data.R` + `_targets_analysis.R`
- Controlled data environment setup script for HCUP restricted data
- Separate data prep and analysis stages
- `{air}` formatted R code

**Data challenge:**

- HCUP is a *controlled* data product (restricted use data agreement)
- Pipeline designed for secure computing environments
- Results exported under data use agreement constraints
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
```
hcup_ap/
├── _targets_data.R      # data prep pipeline
├── _targets_analysis.R  # analysis pipeline
├── R/                   # functions
├── data/                # processed data
└── vignettes/           # documentation
```
:::
:::

---

# Conclusions {.section-title background-color="#1a3a5c"}

## Research Thread Summary

::: {.r-stack}
**A unified program connecting:**
:::

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="25%"}
**Methods**

PrestoGP: scalable spatiotemporal GP + penalized regression
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
**Data**

amadeus: standardized access to 17+ environmental data sources
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
**Models**

beethoven: reproducible national air quality ensemble pipeline
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
**Outcomes**

hcup_ap: wildfire smoke × immune disease epi analysis
:::
:::

. . .

> **Common thread:** Open-source, reproducible, scalable tools that move from exposure measurement to health inference.

## Future Directions

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Exposure modeling:**

- Expand PrestoGP to full exposome panel (multi-pollutant, chemicals)
- Near real-time PM₂.₅ predictions via beethoven
- Sub-daily temporal resolution
- Uncertainty propagation through full epi pipeline

**Health outcomes:**

- Expand beyond wildfire smoke to multi-source air pollution
- Broaden phenotype library
- Causal inference frameworks for ecological analyses
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Open science:**

- beethoven prediction products as public data resource
- amadeus expansion to additional data sources
- PrestoGP: CRAN submission
- Training resources for the epi community

**CHORDS program:**

- Connect exposure tools to EHR and claims data at scale
- Multi-site federated analyses
:::
:::

## Acknowledgments

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**SET Group — NIEHS**

- Insang Song
- Mitchell Manware
- Eva Marques
- Lara Clark
- Mariana Kassien
- Anisha Singh

**Collaborators**

- Matthias Katzfuss (UW Madison)
- Matt Wheeler (NIEHS)
- Cavin Ward-Caviness (EPA)
- Myeongjon Kang (UW Madison)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Funding**

- NIH Intramural Research Program
- NIEHS / CHORDS Program
- K99/R00 ES030993 (Oregon State)

**Data**

- US EPA (AQS, HCUP)
- NASA (MODIS, MERRA-2, SEDAC)
- NOAA (HMS, NARR)
- USGS (GMTED2010)
- AHRQ (HCUP)

*Thank you!*
:::
:::

---

## Questions?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Kyle P. Messier, PhD**  
Stadtman Tenure-Track Investigator  
NIEHS | kyle.messier@nih.gov

**GitHub: `github.com/NIEHS`**

- `NIEHS/PrestoGP` — spatiotemporal GP models
- `NIEHS/amadeus` — environmental data access
- `NIEHS/beethoven` — air quality ensemble pipeline
- `NIEHS/hcup_ap` — wildfire smoke × health outcomes
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
*[QR code to GitHub or slides — placeholder]*
:::
:::
